sentences.text: Life-- life was very hard.
I mean, it's-- nobody took showers over there because we didn't have any showers.
But as-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
--people-- some was-- every-- every-- every day some people went to work.
You know, hard work.
The Russia-- I mean, the-- there was not such a thing as luxuries.
You know?
And if you-- if you-- if you want to take-- to take a bath, I mean, we didn't have, really, any-- we didn't have any showers.
I mean, you know, it was a line for-- a line for to do.
You did not bathe every day.
And-- and the other-- other facilities was-- it-- it was-- it was very tough.
As a child, obviously, you know, I didn't realize it as much as the grownups, because we ran around.
And we didn't-- wanted to believe the life, you know, that-- you know, that's-- we always said the comfort of my parents was there, my-- all my-- my aunts, my cousins.
And it's always like one big happy family, as far as children were concerned.
Well, naturally the grownups, they always had the task of worrying how long it's going to last.
The ghetto was in existence up till September or October of 1942.
About a year, a little-- a little less than a year.
Oh, our winters-- when we-- when we got winter, we got snow till April.
So definitely, winter wasn't-- was-- winter was very, very-- much harder.
And you have to go on-- you have to sneak out to go and chop some wood.
Because if you get caught, you-- you-- you get shot.
But most-- but we survived.
I mean, it's-- it's tough.
And we really burned every-- everything around us possible, except the house, to-- to keep warm.
Because the winters usually, over there-- where I was born last a good deal into April.
Good deal into April.
We had a wood-burning stove.
Yeah, that's-- each-- well, at that time each house had a-- well, a wood-- a wood-burning stove.
The-- the-- the-- that I believe it was like two Ukrainians, two policemen.
I mean, they were Ukrainian.
But there was very few Germans there, though.
It was-- it was-- there was a lieutenant and probably about three assistants.
And everything else was done through the-- through the Ukrainians.
You had your Ukrainian commandant, your Ukraine police.
I-- I think they had one or two, you know, one or two Ukrainians.
And then they had probably like, four or five Yiddish police that were-- that were there.
And they really couldn't do much for you, because if they did something wrong, they get shot.
So it's--
No.
No, no.
They have no-- they had no-- no weapon.
And a-- in the shtetl there-- there, I mean, I'm sure we all heard stories about some of the Yiddish police, but that a not existent where I was born, because everybody knew each other.
And it was just a question of survival.
You know?
And it's-- there's very little they can do for you, or-- or not do for you.
So it's-- we didn't, you know, encounter any problems with the Jewish police, you know, the commandants, whatever.
Like, I'm sure we all heard a lot of stories.
It was nonexistent where I was born.
I'll tell you, we-- I didn't know that-- I didn't know that she was pregnant until the day she gave birth.
None of us-- none of us-- it was the biggest surprise of our life, all right?
I'm sure she didn't.
[LAUGHTER] Because she was on the heavy side.
And I'll tell you, I-- I-- we-- none of us-- if my brother knew, he-- he never let in, or my sister.
And we never, until she gave birth.
And she had-- she gave birth, actually, in 1942.
No.
I would-- I would say it was probably late February.
Because my sister was only seven months old when they liquidated the ghetto.
So if I'm backtracking, I would say probably someplace February or early March.
No, no.
We didn't-- at the place-- at the shtetl where I was born, we-- the-- women gave birth at home.
They have a [? bubbie ?] and then that's how they gave birth.
And--
They helped one another.
That's even before the war.
So it was-- it was normal for them to give birth in the house.
Shocked.
[LAUGHS] I mean, to say the least.
Shocked.
It was happy after that.
She was a very beautiful child.
And I wish it was-- you know, it's-- but it was like, for a while it was a shock.
Well, when they-- when they decided to liquidate the ghetto and it was-- it was around the time of the High Holidays, I believe, you know?
Because something-- it was October, I guess.
And somehow, the word got out.
And a lot of people just took off to the farm and just ran away.
Well, at night, like they say, you know, nobody's going to stab you, you know, not through the gate.
But you know, it-- it was not--
--electrified wires.
No.
I'll tell you, half of the-- half of the population disappeared.
And they postponed it.
They postponed it.
And then people want to-- wanted to believe it's never going to happen.
And when you spend a week in the forest, it rained.
And the fall, you want to believe it's not going to happen.
Oh yeah.
We ran away.
And we ran away.
And my cousin went with us.
And my aunt went with us.
The baby was seven months old, so we couldn't take her.
So we left her with her grandmother.
Halaya, ?] that-- that what survived.
We went not far.
We probably went about five, six kilometer into the forest to hide.
And they postponed it for a week.
And then most of the people came back.
We did not come back.
We stayed in the forest.
There was one of the people that supplied us with food that my father used to go at night to get food told him, he says, I'm going to give you anything you want.
He was a Czech, by the way.
It was not a Ukrainian that time.
And he said, but you are leaving this place tonight.
You cannot come back.
And for a while, we thought he just wants to get rid of us, because he doesn't want to give some more food.
Giving.
At that time, he was giving it to us.
And he says, take whatever you want, whatever you can carry.
But don't come back.
Because you got to leave this-- this part of the forest.
They knew.
Obviously, they knew.
And we left that night.
We went to a different forest, which we really got lost.
I mean, and we-- we-- we-- we walked all night long and probably wound up about six kilometers away, not where we wanted to be.
And that-- that day, they liquidated the ghetto.
They-- they dug three graves on the Jewish cemetery.
And in fact--
The Ukrainians.
I mean, not-- not-- they did not make the Jews do it, this-- this. they-- actually, they-- they dug the graves the week before.
That's why [? there ?] [? wasn't ?] [? anything. ?] And they liquidated.
By liquidated I mean they picked everybody up in the ghetto, whoever was there.
Nobody ran away at that particular time no more.
They made them undress.
They lined them up in-- in-- in their graves and just shot them right across.
We assumed, really, that's how.
We never heard from them.
So we assumed that's-- that's how they die.
Well, we, you know, going back a little bit, my father used to be a peddler.
So he knew a lot of people.
And again, not to be repetitious, he knew a lot of Czechs.
And they were very sympathetic.
And they really gave us, blow for blow, what happened-- what happened, everything.
And that's how we knew exactly what happened.
We knew the amount the graves they did already and how.
They know the whole thing.
And they-- And even the people that ran away-- that ran away, they did not pick them up right away.
But late-- in later days-- a week later, two weeks later-- every time they picked up a family, they let us know who they picked up.
Because they picked up a lot-- a lot-- at that time, I would say, a lot of people ran away.
But one by one, they picked-- over the-- over the next two years, they picked them up, you know.
That night, yes.
And he was 100% correct.
He really meant it-- meant well.
Not that he didn't want-- he really wanted us to get out of there.
Because he figured they're going to, you know, pick everybody up there.
Which they did a week later.
Not that second night, but they did pick everybody up a week later.
Polish.
Polish citizens.
Yeah.
They were big-- they were farmers.
They very had-- very good farms.
I mean, the Ukrainian soil, obviously, we all know-- we know is very-- they were very rich farmers, most of them.
That's why they stayed away and they didn't go back to Czechoslovakia.
Well--
Well, I can recall you one.
First, when we got to the second part of the-- I mean, about-- probably about 12 or 15 kilometers at a different forest, we-- we dug-- what do you call the place to live.
And we were, at that time, seven people.
Let's see, we were four.
We were three, my father was five.
My aunt was six.
My cousin was seven.
And we had another one from the-- from our shtetl and had no relative.
We were eight people.
A woman from our-- you know, she-- she also survived.
I will and later on.
She's still alive--
By absolute coincidence.
Well just you know, when we walked out.
She-- she just latched on and she stayed with us.
Had absolutely no-- no-- it wasn't planned that way.
My cousin, he was-- he was always with me.
Some day since I can remember, he was always with us.
Aaron.
Givintman. ?] And he was always with us.
So it was no-- you know.
So when we went to the different forest, which really was already closing in on the winter.
And-- and we-- and we dug ourselves in there.
And we stayed there for a while.
And-- and we used to go out at night to get food.
My father used to go out at night and get food.
We dug a hole in the ground.
You know, we made the--
A cave, yeah.
Like a cave.
We dug-- we dug in and we-- you know, and that-- that's how we lived, in there.
And-- in fact, after-- after a while we said, you know, eight people in the same place, it's no good, because you have a lot of things.
All-- all of a sudden, you know, there people going to find you.
So we-- we-- we dug another cave about 300, 400 yards away.
And at that cave, myself and my cousin stayed there.
So we were very brave.
Well, then let me tell you.
Let me tell you how brave we were.
So one-- one night, there was a couple of hunters.
And they came-- and they didn't come for us.
They came-- they came to hunt, you know.
And we got so scared.
And we were so brave that we ran-- we ran straight to the other one.
And we led them right there.
But there were not any policemen.
There were hunters.
And one of the-- and one of the guys that was part of the hunters, my father knew.
I mean, it's a-- and he talked them in, to let us come to his barn for a week.
And--
He's one year older.
These guys, one of them he knew.
He talked them in to take us in to his-- to his barn.
Because really, it was getting cold.
It probably was already now, like November.
It was-- it was getting cold.
We were in the forest about six weeks when we encounter.
So ?] [? he ?] [? talked ?] [? us ?] [? in, ?] [? to ?] [? get ?] [? coal. ?] And also, about a mile away from us, there was another family, what also happened to account for eight, eight people.
Because it was the Shamas, the Shamas from shul.
I can't think of his name or anything, but it was the Shamas from shul, with his wife and his kids.
There were eight people.
And that night-- we did not leave that particular night, but we left like a week later.
He talked to me and he came there and says, you can come to my-- to my barn.
That night that we left, they picked up the other family and they shot them all.
We were [? not then known. ?] And that was a big plus for us.
Because there happened to be exactly eight.
And they knew, after the liquidation of the ghetto, how many people we are.
They figured out who is with us and who was missing, they did-- they did not liquidate.
So the word got around that we were killed.
And the--
They keeped--
They kept track who was alive.
Because they knew when the German-- when the Germans came in, certain amount of people went to Russia.
They did not stay.
And so they knew who went there.
And they knew who wasn't killed.
So they kept watch who was-- who was not killed.
And they thought that we were killed.
Because knowing my father, being a little prominent, so they were looking for him.
Now they all knew exactly when we were killed.
And we went to that guy.
And we only stayed there two weeks.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
We were in-- well, we didn't stay together, because it was not good to stay together, for-- just my-- so we-- they stayed probably about a mile away.
Nothing is probably-- is probably the-- nothing is-- is probably the answer.
But just like, delouse our self, if that's the right-- that's about the-- well, we were-- we were kept busy, you know, to keep clean.
But nothing, really.
We had nothing to do.
To this guy for a couple of weeks.
Well, not what-- no, I can't.
Then he-- he didn't want to keep us no more.
Well it was-- it was-- it was actually, if they catch you, they're going to shoot him with us, together.
I mean, it was anybody that saved somebody always, you know, his life was on the line.
That's not my best subject.
[LAUGHS] P-A-W-L-O-S-K-I-- something like that, K-I, something like that.
And he was a very poor farmer.
I think he had six kids himself.
I think he had like, four acres of land.
I mean, that was it.
And talked him in.
And beside talking him in, he also-- we had some-- I don't know if it was money, or if it was just some cloth.
Ukraine.
He was Ukraine.
More sympathetic.
And he took us in.
Well, he build us-- our-- a-- in-- in the barn, he build like a-- a-- another small floor, something, to keep us.
You know?
And he really hid it on top-- on top of the barn.
He separated a-- he made a room out of it.
Up above.
And he took us in, but he couldn't feed us, because really, he really was a very poor guy.
But then again, with the contacts my father had, so we used to go out once a week.
Not so much myself, my brother.
I went a few times, but mainly my brother and my father, when he was alive, used to go out once a week and bring food from a different guy that really supplied us.
The other guy was a Czech.
Not a Czech farmer, not the same guy.
They used to-- and he really fed us.
Every week they used to go out and they used to prepare food and-- and bring us.
And we used to-- that's how we lived over there.
Well, we really existed mostly, you know-- in the winter, it's not-- it's not really that warm of weather there.
So it's-- things don't really spoil overnight.
In the winter months, it was no problem.
I mean, it was cold enough there.
[LAUGHS]
No.
No, no, no, no.
It was-- well, with-- in there with straw and blankets, really.
There was no-- no fire, nothing.
We couldn't be seen.
We-- we-- we couldn't be heard.
Because we you know, really, that was very, very important.
Couldn't be seen and couldn't be heard.
But even part of his family, what they had one kid was very young, he didn't know we were there.
Yeah, I don't know, they--
--were old enough there, they know we are-- that we were there.
And then-- and then of-- around Christmastime of '42, my father died.
Well, we assume he had an ulcer.
So we assume it-- it started bleeding and he died from it.
I mean, it's all-- we-- we all assume that.
And we buried them ourselves in that barn at that time.
Well, it was in the-- we buried him in the barn.
So yeah.
It was, well, we buried-- we buried him our self.
My-- my brother, myself, and-- and that was around Christmas in 1942.
But he-- he kept us-- we were at that time, because the-- our big worry was they're going to throw us out.
But he did not.
He kept us through '43, with the same system.
We used to go out-- we used to go out to get food.
Mainly my brother used to go, and my aunt used to go.
And like I say, once in a while, myself.
And it was probably about five-- four or five kilometers.
We used to go at night.
Into the-- you know, and the guy used to prepare the food.
We used to-- Where was-- where would he be?
At his house?
He was at his house.
We used to go out to his house.
We didn't-- we used to go his house.
And that's how we survived all through '43, into February of '44.
And that's how long you were in hiding?
That's how long we were in hiding.
Now, what did you do for toilet or water?
And who-- and who would take care of that?
At night.
You know?
You-- you-- you stayed with it all day long.
At night-- at-- at night, you cleaned it out.
Just a bucket, you know.
We, actually-- during the day we didn't-- we didn't move.
Because there was no room to move.
I mean, the-- the place they put us up, they-- they built a mock room for eight people.
It was-- it was not enough, really.
You couldn't even walk.
I mean, really, to tell you, we used to do that at night.
We used to go down to the barn.
You know, stretch-- stretch your legs and walk and clean out everything.
You know?
It's--
It was worse than-- I mean, at that time, obviously, it was the-- it was great for us just to-- just to-- be-- just to live.
But it was-- the conditions was deplorable, I mean.
I know that he made it.
He did everything he could.
But it-- it was really-- he did everything he could.
Petro Pawlotzki and his wife, I mean too-- and he really risked his life.
He really risked his life.
We had-- yes.
We had contact with him after the-- so in fact, after World War I it was the-- when the Russian came back, that they used to pick up all-- they drafted all the men right away.
Because my brother went into the army too, at that time.
It was the--
Yeah.
They-- they-- and-- and what they did, they also picked up a lot of women and sent them-- and sent them to work camps.
So his daughter was one of them.
So when-- when we-- we brought it to her house when we lived in Rowne already.
And she got a job, so they let-- they let her-- they did not send her away to-- to-- to work her.
Yes.
Oh yeah.
While we were there, we always had contact.
We had contacts.
Even when we live over here we send them packages.
We used to send them most-- you know, they-- they would resell over there.
But they both passed away already.
They were much older.
Yeah, we had contact with them probably through early '80s.
Well, we would be there.
I don't know, sleep.
I mean, we had enough-- you know-- and really, we'd just sit and keep quiet and whisper.
I mean, you couldn't really talk.
And his closest neighbor, I'm sure, was a half-- a quarter mile away or something.
But you-- people, you know, they worked.
They came.
So you really-- all day long you didn't say a word.
So--
Oh, yeah.
It's--
Oh, yeah.
A lot of animals they stored, yeah.
Yeah, you probably had a half-a-dozen cows.
And that's how you survived.
And I think he had four horses, if I'm-- if I'm correct.
A lot of bread.
A lot of bread.
And really, a-- a lot of bread and a lot of kielbasa, salami, like, you know, a lot-- a lot-- a lot of that.
Kosher?
[LAUGHS]
Including [YIDDISH], don't worry, there was that too.
Yes.
I-- I really don't know how my-- how my mother or father did it, but they knew the Jewish holidays.
They-- somehow they knew when it was, yes.
I can't even really answer you how they knew, but they knew when it was.
When it was-- and we were there a whole-- all of 1943.
They knew when Pesach was.
I don't know that they did anything.
It didn't mean anything.
But they knew, you know, it's Pesach, it's Shavuot, and it's the High Holiday.
Somehow, they knew.
No.
My-- before my mother-- my father died, he used to say-- he used to cry.
He used to say, the only-- he used to look at me and say, he's the only guy who hasn't lived yet.
But in fact, he was not-- he was already-- he died a year before I was bar mitzvahed So I was never bar mitzvahed, in fact.
I was never bar mitzvahed for a long time.
I was not bar mitzvahed till-- till 1993.
My granddaughter.
At Temple Torah.
It's on-- I think that's in the Temple Torah, right-- where my granddaughter went.
You know, it was not at my temple.
It's in Dix Hills.
Well, we had plenty-- plenty of problems, you know, it being-- being such a close quarters.
Everybody, you know, it's-- but somehow we survived.
I mean, there's no question about it.
You know, everybody gets on each other's nerves.
And there's only one problem.
You couldn't leave.
[LAUGHS] There was no place to go.
But the normal, natural problems, you know, of every day.
Somewhat.
I think we probably came closer.
Because we-- everybody thought, you know, we don't have the guy that's going to take care of us.
Everybody looks up to my father.
So when he died, that probably made us closer, you know?
To-- everybody started worrying about more than before.
Before it was well, he's going to take care of the-- you know, the image.
Even though it was really out of his hands to do anything anymore, well, you know, you see.
But somehow you feel in your head, he's-- he's going to-- he's going to go you know, bring bread.
He's going to go-- he's going to-- if he's-- if guy throws us out, you know, he'll dig up another guy who's going to save you.
But after that, it was-- it was really scary.
Because we know if this guy says-- comes down one and says, leave, where are you going to go?
And we knew the guy that-- that kept giving us the food.
He'd do anything for you.
But he cannot really take you in there.
And we knew that.
Because-- because they were really watching the Czechs, you know.
They knew, if anybody's going to-- if anybody was going to hide anybody it was going to be them.
Again, they didn't look for us.
It was-- it was-- I mean, I know they-- they kill eight people.
But if they would-- if they wouldn't think that we were alive, they would have dug us up.
They would have find us somehow.
No, because they obviously didn't care.
They-- they-- probably-- somehow, they thought-- because when I came out in 1944, and I met one of my cousins that survived on her own, she was shocked.
She says, you can't be alive.
Because I know where you die-- I know-- I know where you're buried.
She thought-- because she couldn't believe it.
Because they-- everybody knew you guys died, you know, when we died and where we were buried.
And it was him.
It was the Shamas with his-- with his family.
I would say probably, you know, they-- because they stopped looking for us.
They really accounted for everybody in the shtetl that was missing.
She was also in hiding.
She was also in hiding.
Yeah, she was in hiding.
She survived.
I didn't even-- we didn't know she was alive till we came back.
We did not know she was alive.
He was 40.
It was 1902.
He died at the end of 1942.
He was 40.
They're about the same age, months apart.
My-- my-- my mother's birthday is June-- June 5th, let's say.
[LAUGHS]
My mother did survive.
And she lived to a ripe age.
She died when she was 90 and a 1/2.
So she lived a long life.
OK.
